subtopic_detail: Initial workup of weight faltering in 9-month-old without organic red flags
stem: You are the GP reviewing a 9-month-old boy whose weight has dropped from the 25th to the 3rd centile across the last three reviews. He was born at term, weighed 3.2 kg, and is exclusively breastfed with one daily solids tasting. His mother describes long unsettled feeds and reports she eats no extra protein. He smiles, transfers objects, and sits unsupported. There is no diarrhoea, vomiting, recurrent infection, or family history of coeliac disease.

data_table.Growth: Weight 6.4 kg (3rd centile, dropping), length 70 cm (25th), head circ 44 cm (25th)
data_table.Examination: Alert, no dysmorphism, no organomegaly, normal cardiac exam, soft non-distended abdomen, no rashes
data_table.Feeding: Breast feeds 6-8 times daily; offered pureed vegetable and rice cereal once daily; no formula

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer for paediatric admission and inpatient nasogastric supplemental feeding trial
options.rationale: Admission and NG feeds are reserved for severe weight faltering with dehydration, organic disease, or failed community escalation; this child is alert and developing normally. (Source: RCH CPG: Faltering growth)

options.text: Arrange a structured feeding history with three-day diet diary and observe a feed
options.rationale: Detailed feeding assessment and observed feed remain the first-line evaluation for non-organic weight faltering before any investigation cascade. (Source: RCH CPG: Faltering growth)

options.text: Order coeliac serology, full blood count, and renal function as the next investigation
options.rationale: Targeted bloods are reserved for children with red flags or no improvement after dietary and feeding optimisation; this child has none. (Source: RCH CPG: Faltering growth)

options.text: Advise immediate cessation of breastfeeding and introduction of full-strength formula
options.rationale: There is no evidence-based reason to stop breastfeeding; mixed feeding decisions follow assessment, not precede it. (Source: NHMRC Infant Feeding Guidelines)

options.text: Reassure the mother that weight tracking on the 3rd centile is normal variation
options.rationale: Crossing two major centile lines downward warrants assessment rather than reassurance, even when development is preserved. (Source: RCH CPG: Faltering growth)

explanation.summary: Weight faltering in infancy is most commonly multifactorial, with inadequate intake the leading cause when there are no organic red flags. Evaluation starts with a feeding history, three-day diet diary, and observation of a feed before laboratory testing. Investigations are guided by red flags such as diarrhoea, vomiting, recurrent infection, dysmorphism, or developmental regression. Solids should be established with iron-rich foods around six months in addition to milk feeds.
key_points: Crossing two centiles downward triggers structured assessment
Feeding observation and diet diary precede investigations
Coeliac and other serology reserved for red flags or non-response
NG feeds reserved for severe or unresponsive cases
explanation.pearls: An observed breastfeed in clinic typically lasts 10 to 40 minutes; transfer volumes under 60 mL in a 9 month old over a sustained feed flag low effective intake before any blood test is sent.

subtopic_detail: HPV vaccine routine schedule for 12-year-old at school-based program
stem: You are the school-based immunisation doctor reviewing a 12-year-old girl in Year 7 attending the routine school program. She is fit, well, has had no severe vaccine reactions, and is not immunocompromised. Her mother has consented in writing and asks whether she will need a second dose later this year or next.

data_table.Examination: Well, no acute illness, BMI on the 60th centile
data_table.Immunisations: Up to date with childhood schedule; never received HPV
data_table.Other: No pregnancy, no immunosuppression, no current febrile illness

lead_in: What is the most appropriate HPV immunisation plan?

options.text: Administer Gardasil 9 today and book a second dose at six months
options.rationale: The two-dose schedule was replaced in 2023 by a single-dose schedule for healthy adolescents aged 9 to under 26 years. (Source: ATAGI; Australian Immunisation Handbook)

options.text: Administer Gardasil 9 today as a single dose with no further doses required
options.rationale: Healthy adolescents aged 9 to under 26 years receive a single dose of Gardasil 9 under the AU schedule since 2023. (Source: ATAGI; Australian Immunisation Handbook)

options.text: Defer vaccination until she turns 14 years to align with menarche cohort outcomes
options.rationale: There is no clinical reason to delay; immunogenicity and program uptake are highest in early Year 7. (Source: ATAGI; Australian Immunisation Handbook)

options.text: Administer Gardasil 9 today and book three total doses across six months
options.rationale: Three doses are only required for moderately to severely immunocompromised adolescents, not healthy 12-year-olds. (Source: ATAGI; Australian Immunisation Handbook)

options.text: Decline routine vaccination and instead recommend cervical screening once eligible
options.rationale: Vaccination and screening are complementary; vaccination prevents most oncogenic HPV exposure that screening later detects. (Source: NCSP; Cancer Council Australia)

explanation.summary: The Australian National Immunisation Program moved to a single-dose Gardasil 9 schedule for healthy adolescents aged 9 to under 26 years in 2023. Three doses remain for those with moderate to severe immunocompromise. The program is delivered in Year 7 to maximise pre-exposure efficacy and uptake. Vaccination does not replace cervical screening, which begins at age 25 under the renewed National Cervical Screening Program.
key_points: Single-dose Gardasil 9 for healthy 9 to under 26 years
Three doses retained for moderate to severe immunocompromise
Delivered in Year 7 by school-based programs
Does not replace cervical screening from age 25
explanation.pearls: Gardasil 9 covers HPV types 6, 11, 16, 18, 31, 33, 45, 52, and 58 and is the only HPV vaccine available on the Australian schedule since 2018.

subtopic_detail: Differentiating paraphimosis from physiological phimosis in 4-year-old boy
stem: You are the ED registrar reviewing a 4-year-old boy whose father retracted his foreskin during a bath two hours ago to clean underneath. The foreskin has not returned over the glans and the child is now crying with marked penile discomfort. There has been no trauma, fever, or urinary symptoms, and he passes urine without difficulty. The father describes the foreskin as previously non-retractable until tonight.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8, HR 124, RR 24, BP 96/60, SpO2 99 percent
data_table.Examination: Foreskin retracted behind a swollen, painful glans with a constricting band; glans pink, warm, non-dusky; no urinary retention
data_table.Past history: Otherwise well, immunisations up to date, no surgical history

lead_in: What is the single best initial intervention?

options.text: Reassure the family that the foreskin is non-retractable and arrange GP review
options.rationale: This is paraphimosis with an entrapped foreskin, which is a urological emergency rather than physiological phimosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreskin conditions)

options.text: Apply topical lignocaine gel and attempt manual reduction with sustained glans compression
options.rationale: Topical anaesthesia with sustained glans compression to reduce oedema followed by manual reduction is the first-line management of paraphimosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreskin conditions)

options.text: Refer to urology for elective circumcision booking and discharge with paracetamol
options.rationale: Definitive surgery is not the first step; the entrapment must be reduced urgently to avoid glans ischaemia. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreskin conditions)

options.text: Apply topical corticosteroid cream twice daily for six weeks and review in clinic
options.rationale: Topical steroid is treatment for symptomatic phimosis, not for an acute paraphimosis with entrapment. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreskin conditions)

options.text: Insert urinary catheter to relieve urinary retention and admit for intravenous antibiotics
options.rationale: There is no retention or infection; catheterisation does not address the foreskin entrapment. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreskin conditions)

explanation.summary: Paraphimosis occurs when a retracted foreskin cannot be returned over the glans and creates a constricting band leading to oedema and risk of ischaemia. Initial management uses topical anaesthesia, sustained manual compression of the glans to reduce oedema, and gentle reduction. If reduction fails or the glans becomes dusky, urgent urology review for dorsal slit is required. This contrasts with physiological phimosis, where the foreskin is simply non-retractable in a well child without entrapment.
key_points: Paraphimosis is a urological emergency, not phimosis
First step: topical anaesthesia and manual reduction
Failed reduction or dusky glans needs urgent urology review
Never forcibly retract a physiologically tight foreskin
explanation.pearls: Most physiological phimosis resolves by age 10 without any intervention; only 1 percent of boys still have a non-retractile foreskin at age 16.

sources.label: Urological Society of Australia and New Zealand

subtopic_detail: Suspected foreign body inhalation in toddler with focal wheeze and stable airway
stem: You are the paediatric ED registrar reviewing an 18-month-old girl who began coughing during a family dinner two hours ago. Her grandmother saw her chewing peanuts before the choking episode. She is now alert, tolerating sips, with mild intermittent cough but no stridor or cyanosis. On auscultation she has decreased air entry and end-expiratory wheeze localised to the right side.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9, HR 132, RR 32, SpO2 96 percent on air, BP 98/60
data_table.Examination: Sitting comfortably, no tripod posture, mild intercostal recession, focal right wheeze and reduced air entry
data_table.Investigations: Chest X-ray inspiration: subtle right hyperinflation; expiration film: persistent right hyperinflation with mediastinal shift to the left
data_table.Past history: Well child, full immunisation, no asthma

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Trial salbutamol via spacer and review in two hours before any procedural plan
options.rationale: Bronchodilators do not move a fixed obstructing foreign body and may delay definitive removal. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign body inhalation)

options.text: Refer urgently to ENT or paediatric surgery for rigid bronchoscopy under general anaesthesia
options.rationale: Rigid bronchoscopy under general anaesthesia is the definitive intervention for suspected airway foreign body with focal signs and air trapping. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign body inhalation)

options.text: Perform five back blows and five chest thrusts and admit for observation overnight
options.rationale: Back blows are part of basic life support for complete airway obstruction, not for a stable child with focal signs. (Source: ANZCOR Guideline 4)

options.text: Order high-resolution chest CT to confirm radiolucent foreign body before any referral
options.rationale: CT may delay definitive bronchoscopy when the clinical picture and inspiration-expiration films already support a foreign body. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign body inhalation)

options.text: Discharge home with chest physiotherapy advice and GP review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Discharge risks delayed pneumonia, atelectasis, and irreversible airway injury from a retained foreign body. (Source: RCH CPG: Foreign body inhalation)

explanation.summary: Suspected airway foreign body in a toddler with witnessed choking, focal wheeze, and unilateral air trapping on inspiration-expiration films is a definitive indication for rigid bronchoscopy under general anaesthesia. Bronchodilators, back blows, and CT all delay treatment. Children with peanut, seed, or hard food inhalation can present with a quiet asymptomatic phase before secondary pneumonia. Initial assessment focuses on whether the airway is patent and whether the child can phonate and cough effectively.
key_points: Witnessed choking with focal findings = bronchoscopy
Expiration film air trapping is a key radiological clue
Back blows only for complete obstruction in a basic life support sequence
Avoid bronchodilators or CT delays
explanation.pearls: Right main bronchus is the most common site for inhaled peanut in toddlers because of its more vertical orientation; ipsilateral air trapping on expiration film is the most sensitive radiographic sign.

subtopic_detail: Suspected button battery oesophageal ingestion in 2-year-old with delayed presentation
stem: You are the rural ED consultant reviewing a 2-year-old boy whose mother found him near a hearing-aid box six hours ago. He vomited once, refused dinner, and is now drooling with retrosternal discomfort. The nearest endoscopy service is at a tertiary hospital 90 minutes away by road retrieval. He has no stridor, intact saturation, and looks well perfused. The mother offered him milk to drink an hour ago; he kept it down.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9, HR 134, RR 30, SpO2 99 percent on air, BP 98/56
data_table.Examination: Drooling, refusing oral intake, mildly distressed; chest clear; no haemoptysis; abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: Chest and abdominal X-ray: 20 mm circular radiopaque object with double-rim halo lodged at the level of the thoracic inlet; no free air; no pneumomediastinum

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next intervention before retrieval?

options.text: Administer 10 mL aliquots of oral honey every 10 minutes while awaiting retrieval transport
options.rationale: Oral honey within 12 hours of ingestion in a child over 12 months reduces oesophageal alkali injury severity pending endoscopic removal. (Source: RCH CPG: Button battery; NCPP)

options.text: Withhold all oral intake and arrange immediate endoscopic removal at the local hospital
options.rationale: Local endoscopy is not available; oral nil by mouth alone offers no tissue protection during the transport delay. (Source: RCH CPG: Button battery)

options.text: Induce vomiting with syrup of ipecac to attempt expulsion before transfer to the tertiary centre
options.rationale: Induced vomiting risks aspiration, further mucosal injury, and is contraindicated for button battery ingestion. (Source: NSW Poisons Information Centre)

options.text: Give oral activated charcoal 1 g/kg via cup to bind the battery contents before retrieval departure
options.rationale: Charcoal does not bind battery alkali leakage and adds aspiration risk without altering tissue injury. (Source: NSW Poisons Information Centre)

options.text: Insert a wide-bore nasogastric tube and attempt mechanical dislodgement of the battery toward the stomach
options.rationale: Blind NG manipulation risks oesophageal perforation and is not part of any current guideline. (Source: RCH CPG: Button battery)

explanation.summary: Oesophageal button battery is a time-critical emergency because alkali generation begins within two hours and full-thickness injury within four hours. Australian guidance supports oral honey 10 mL every 10 minutes in children older than 12 months presenting within 12 hours, awaiting endoscopic removal as the definitive treatment. Sucralfate suspension is an alternative in hospital. Imaging shows the characteristic double-rim halo on PA view and step-off on lateral, distinguishing battery from coin.
key_points: Time-critical: tissue injury within 2 to 4 hours
Honey 10 mL every 10 min if over 12 months and within 12 hours
Definitive treatment is endoscopic removal under anaesthesia
Never induce vomiting or attempt blind NG dislodgement
explanation.pearls: On PA chest X-ray a button battery shows a double-rim halo whereas a coin shows a single uniform edge; the lateral film shows a step-off at the negative pole that points to the side of greatest mucosal injury.

subtopic_detail: First-line investigation for global developmental delay with no syndromic features
stem: You are the paediatrician reviewing a 3-year-old boy referred for delay in all developmental domains. He is non-verbal, walks unsteadily, and has poor social engagement. There is no parental consanguinity, no regression, no seizures, and no dysmorphism. Hearing and vision testing are normal. Newborn screening was unremarkable and no metabolic decompensation history exists. Family is keen to identify a cause to guide reproductive counselling.

data_table.Growth: Weight 75th centile, height 70th centile, OFC 50th centile
data_table.Examination: Non-dysmorphic, normal cardiac and abdominal examination, normal genitalia, normal skin findings
data_table.Investigations: TSH, ferritin, creatine kinase, urine metabolic screen all normal; MRI brain not yet done

lead_in: What is the single best next investigation?

options.text: Karyotype with G-banding to screen for whole chromosome aneuploidy as a first step
options.rationale: Karyotype is reserved for suspected Down or Turner syndrome; it has lower yield than microarray for non-syndromic delay. (Source: RACP; HGSA)
source_refs: HGSA: Genetic testing in developmental delay

options.text: Chromosomal microarray as the first-tier genetic investigation for unexplained global delay
options.rationale: Chromosomal microarray is the first-line genetic test for unexplained global developmental delay or intellectual disability and detects copy number variants with about 15 percent yield. (Source: RACP; HGSA)
source_refs: HGSA: Genetic testing in developmental delay

options.text: MRI brain with contrast as the highest yield single investigation in this clinical setting
options.rationale: MRI is useful if there are focal neurological findings, microcephaly, regression, or seizures; otherwise yield is lower than microarray. (Source: RACP)

options.text: Whole exome sequencing as the first genetic test to bypass microarray triage entirely
options.rationale: Exome is a second-tier test in Australia after a normal microarray and fragile X, or when a specific syndrome is suspected. (Source: HGSA)
source_refs: HGSA: Genetic testing in developmental delay

options.text: EEG to exclude epileptic encephalopathy as the underlying cause of the delay
options.rationale: EEG is reserved for suspected seizures or regression; routine EEG is not part of the workup for global delay. (Source: RACP)

explanation.summary: Global developmental delay in a child without dysmorphism, regression, or seizures still has a high yield genetic basis. Chromosomal microarray is the first-tier genetic investigation in Australia, detecting clinically significant copy number variants in approximately 15 percent of unexplained cases. Fragile X testing is concurrent or sequential. Exome sequencing follows if first-tier testing is negative or if specific syndromic features emerge. Neuroimaging is reserved for focal neurological signs, microcephaly, regression, or seizures.
key_points: Microarray is first-tier for unexplained global delay
Fragile X testing is concurrent or sequential
Exome sequencing is second-tier after negative first-tier
Neuroimaging only if focal signs, microcephaly, regression, or seizures
explanation.pearls: Chromosomal microarray has a diagnostic yield of about 15 percent in non-syndromic global developmental delay versus about 3 percent for karyotype, which is why it has replaced karyotype as first-tier in Australian practice.

sources.label: HGSA: Genetic testing in developmental delay

subtopic_detail: Postdates pregnancy at 41+0 weeks with favourable cervix and reassuring CTG
stem: You are the antenatal clinic registrar reviewing a 32-year-old primigravida at 41+0 weeks. The pregnancy has been uncomplicated, the fetus is in cephalic presentation, and she has had reassuring CTGs at her last two appointments. Bishop score today is 8, she has no contraindications to vaginal birth, and she has read written information about postdates options. She asks what the recommended next step is.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7, HR 80, RR 16, BP 118/74, SpO2 99 percent
data_table.Pregnancy: Singleton, cephalic, EFW 50th centile, fundal height appropriate, fetal movements normal
data_table.Investigations: CTG reactive; AFI normal; group B strep swab negative; bloods within normal limits
data_table.Bishop score: 8 (cervix 2 cm dilated, soft, anterior, 80 percent effaced, head at -1)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next recommendation?

options.text: Recommend expectant management with twice weekly CTG until spontaneous labour by 43 weeks
options.rationale: Stillbirth and meconium aspiration risk rise after 41 weeks and become significant beyond 42 weeks; expectant management to 43 weeks is not recommended. (Source: RANZCOG: Induction of labour)

options.text: Recommend induction of labour between 41+0 and 41+3 weeks with amniotomy plus oxytocin
options.rationale: Induction at 41+0 to 41+3 weeks reduces perinatal mortality and meconium aspiration with no increase in caesarean rate, and amniotomy plus oxytocin suits a favourable cervix. (Source: RANZCOG: Induction of labour)

options.text: Recommend immediate elective caesarean section at 41+0 weeks for postdates indication
options.rationale: Postdates alone is not an indication for caesarean when the cervix is favourable and the fetus is well. (Source: RANZCOG: Induction of labour)

options.text: Recommend cervical Foley balloon insertion as a mechanical ripening method tonight
options.rationale: Mechanical ripening is reserved for unfavourable cervix; with Bishop score 8 the cervix is already favourable. (Source: RANZCOG: Induction of labour)

options.text: Recommend a stretch and sweep at clinic today and return in seven days for review
options.rationale: A stretch and sweep alone delays definitive induction; perinatal risk continues to rise during that interval. (Source: RANZCOG: Induction of labour)

explanation.summary: Induction of labour between 41+0 and 41+3 weeks reduces perinatal mortality, meconium aspiration, and the need for emergency caesarean compared with expectant management. The method is chosen on Bishop score: amniotomy plus oxytocin for favourable cervix and prostaglandin or balloon for unfavourable cervix. Postdates alone is not an indication for caesarean. Counselling addresses the small absolute mortality reduction and the practical implications of a planned admission.
key_points: 41+0 to 41+3 weeks is the routine induction window
Bishop score guides method choice
Favourable cervix: amniotomy plus oxytocin
Unfavourable cervix: PG or balloon then amniotomy
explanation.pearls: Routine induction at 41 weeks reduces perinatal mortality from about 3 to 0.4 per 1000 with no increase in caesarean rates, which is the basis for the RANZCOG default 41+0 to 41+3 window.

subtopic_detail: Painful latch assessment in established breastfeeding at day 5 postpartum
stem: You are the postnatal ward registrar reviewing a 28-year-old primipara on day 5 after spontaneous vaginal birth. She is breastfeeding every two to three hours but reports sharp nipple pain throughout the feed and now has cracked nipples. The baby is alert, producing five wet nappies in 24 hours, and has lost 6 percent of birth weight. There is no maternal fever, no breast erythema, and no induration. On observation the baby latches with pursed lips and an audible click during sucking.

data_table.Breast examination: Both nipples with linear cracks at base, no erythema, no induration, no purulent discharge
data_table.Infant observation: Latch shallow with pursed lips and clicking; tongue elevated; no audible swallowing
data_table.Investigations: No swabs taken; baby afebrile and feeding

lead_in: What is the most appropriate single next step?

options.text: Commence empiric oral flucloxacillin and topical mupirocin for presumptive nipple infection
options.rationale: There is no fever, erythema, induration, or purulent discharge; antibiotics are not first-line for painful latch alone. (Source: ABA; Therapeutic Guidelines)

options.text: Arrange immediate referral for frenotomy to release a presumed posterior tongue tie
options.rationale: Frenotomy is considered only after thorough lactation assessment with failure of optimised positioning, not as the first step. (Source: ABA: Breastfeeding)

options.text: Refer to lactation consultant for positioning and attachment review with feed observation
options.rationale: Painful latch with shallow attachment is most often a positioning and attachment problem that benefits from structured lactation consultant review. (Source: ABA; RANZCOG)

options.text: Recommend cessation of breastfeeding and transition to exclusive formula bottle feeds
options.rationale: Cessation is premature; mother and baby benefit from troubleshooting before stopping breastfeeding. (Source: NHMRC Infant Feeding Guidelines)

options.text: Recommend nipple shields for one week to allow the cracked nipples to heal completely
options.rationale: Nipple shields are a short-term adjunct, not a substitute for fixing positioning; long-term use can reduce milk transfer. (Source: ABA)

explanation.summary: Painful latch and cracked nipples in early breastfeeding most often reflect positioning and attachment problems rather than infection or tongue tie. First-line management is structured lactation consultant assessment with observed feed and correction of attachment. Antibiotics are reserved for confirmed infection. Frenotomy is considered only after lactation review with failure of optimised positioning. Weight loss up to 7 to 10 percent in the first week is within normal limits.
key_points: Painful latch usually due to attachment, not infection
Lactation consultant review with feed observation is first-line
Antibiotics only if signs of infection
Frenotomy only after lactation optimisation
explanation.pearls: Audible clicking during feeds typically indicates poor seal from shallow attachment; correcting the latch frequently resolves both nipple pain and click without any device or surgical intervention.

subtopic_detail: Suitability for external cephalic version at 37 weeks for breech presentation
stem: You are the obstetric registrar reviewing a 31-year-old primigravida at 37+0 weeks whose presentation scan today confirms a frank breech. The pregnancy is otherwise uncomplicated with a single fetus, normal liquor volume, and an anterior placenta away from the lower segment. She has had no antepartum haemorrhage, no scars on her uterus, and intact membranes. She prefers vaginal birth and asks about external cephalic version.

data_table.Pregnancy: Singleton, frank breech, EFW 50th centile, AFI 12, anterior placenta clear of lower segment
data_table.Investigations: CTG reactive; bloods within normal limits; no antepartum haemorrhage
data_table.Past history: Nil regular medication, no uterine surgery, BMI 24

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Plan elective caesarean at 39 weeks without offering external cephalic version trial
options.rationale: ECV should be offered to all eligible women with breech at term as it reduces caesarean rates compared with no ECV. (Source: RANZCOG: ECV)

options.text: Offer external cephalic version with tocolysis and CTG monitoring in a delivery unit
options.rationale: ECV at 37 weeks with terbutaline tocolysis and CTG monitoring is the recommended approach for uncomplicated term breech. (Source: RANZCOG: ECV)

options.text: Plan vaginal breech birth without offering external cephalic version this admission
options.rationale: Vaginal breech birth is an option but should not be offered instead of ECV; the choice is sequential. (Source: RANZCOG: ECV)

options.text: Recommend moxibustion and knee-chest exercises at home with rescan in two weeks time
options.rationale: Complementary techniques have low-quality evidence and delay definitive ECV; they are not the standard first option. (Source: RANZCOG: ECV)

options.text: Schedule repeat scan at 39 weeks to confirm presentation before any plan is offered
options.rationale: Waiting until 39 weeks misses the optimal window for ECV and increases the chance of breech caesarean by default. (Source: RANZCOG: ECV)

explanation.summary: External cephalic version at 37 weeks is recommended for uncomplicated singleton breech because it reduces the rate of caesarean birth. Contraindications include placenta praevia, antepartum haemorrhage, ruptured membranes, multiple pregnancy, uterine scar where ECV is locally contraindicated, fetal compromise, and oligohydramnios. The procedure is performed in a unit with CTG monitoring, theatre access, and tocolysis with terbutaline. Anti-D is given to Rhesus negative women.
key_points: Offer ECV from 37 weeks for uncomplicated breech
Tocolysis with terbutaline improves success
Done with CTG monitoring and theatre access
Anti-D given if Rhesus negative
explanation.pearls: Successful ECV rates are around 50 percent overall but higher in multiparous women with relaxed abdomen, posterior placenta, and ample liquor; success approaches 70 percent in those subgroups.

subtopic_detail: Sudden fresh per vaginal bleeding at ARM with confirmed vasa praevia on antenatal scan
stem: You are the obstetric registrar in birth suite with a 33-year-old multipara at 36+2 weeks admitted for planned caesarean tomorrow for known vasa praevia diagnosed on antenatal transvaginal scan. She develops fresh vaginal bleeding 50 mL after spontaneous rupture of membranes during admission. CTG shows a sinusoidal pattern with reduced variability. There is no maternal hypotension, no abdominal pain, and no uterine tenderness.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8, HR 92, RR 18, BP 116/70, SpO2 99 percent
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, fresh blood at the introitus 50 mL, cervix 3 cm dilated, vertex high
data_table.Investigations: CTG sinusoidal with reduced variability; group and hold valid; bloods including coagulation in progress
data_table.Antenatal scan: Confirmed vasa praevia with fetal vessels traversing the internal os 1 cm above the cervix

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next intervention?

options.text: Proceed immediately to category 1 caesarean section in theatre as a fetal emergency
options.rationale: Sinusoidal CTG with fresh PV bleeding in known vasa praevia indicates fetal exsanguination and mandates immediate category 1 caesarean. (Source: RANZCOG: Vasa praevia)

options.text: Transfuse two units of O-negative red cells to the mother and reassess after 30 minutes
options.rationale: The bleeding is fetal not maternal; maternal transfusion does not address fetal exsanguination. (Source: RANZCOG: Vasa praevia)

options.text: Start intravenous magnesium sulfate for fetal neuroprotection before any delivery decision
options.rationale: Magnesium delays delivery; in a category 1 fetal emergency the priority is delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Magnesium for neuroprotection)

options.text: Administer betamethasone 11.4 mg IM and plan caesarean in 24 hours after lung maturation
options.rationale: Steroid course takes 24 to 48 hours and cannot be awaited when fetal exsanguination is underway. (Source: RANZCOG: Antenatal corticosteroids)

options.text: Continue intrapartum monitoring with continuous CTG and aim for vaginal birth with vacuum
options.rationale: Vaginal birth in confirmed vasa praevia with bleeding has a high fetal mortality and is contraindicated. (Source: RANZCOG: Vasa praevia)

explanation.summary: Vasa praevia carries a high perinatal mortality if undiagnosed because membrane rupture tears unprotected fetal vessels crossing the cervix. The Apt or alkali denaturation test can confirm fetal origin of blood in stable cases. When CTG shows a sinusoidal pattern with fresh bleeding the diagnosis is fetal exsanguination and category 1 caesarean within 30 minutes is mandatory. Antenatal diagnosis with planned caesarean at 34 to 36 weeks before spontaneous labour is the prevention strategy.
key_points: Sinusoidal CTG plus PV bleeding equals fetal exsanguination
Category 1 caesarean within 30 minutes
Steroids and magnesium are not relevant in active fetal compromise
Antenatal diagnosis enables planned caesarean at 34 to 36 weeks
explanation.pearls: Fetal blood volume at term is only about 80 mL/kg so even 100 mL of fresh PV bleeding from vasa praevia can exsanguinate a term infant in minutes; the sinusoidal CTG is a late and ominous sign.
